Taravangian
Taravangian
Fan art by sheep
[1]
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Frostlandian
Nationality
Frostlandian
Gender
Male
Abilities
Shardholder
(
Odium
)
Physical Description
Hair Color
White
Eye Color
Pale grey
Social Information
Title(s)
King of
Kharbranth
,
King of
Jah Keved
(formerly)
Family
Savrahalidem
(daughter),
Unnamed Child (
granddaughter
),
Gvori
(granddaughter),
Karavaniga
(granddaughter),
Ruli
(granddaughter),
Occupation
King (formerly), Prisoner (formerly), Shardholder
Appears in
The Way of Kings
,
Words of Radiance
,
Oathbringer
,
Rhythm of War
Taravangian
was the King of Kharbranth during The Way of Kings, and is now also the King of Jah Keved, given the events within Words of Radiance. Publicly, he appears to be a kindly, philanthropic, but ultimately dim man. However, these features are merely skin deep, as he hides dark secrets behind this visage.
Appearance
Shallan
initially describes Taravangian as elderly, with a wispy white beard, pale grey eyes, an open and kindly face, and as wearing royal orange robes.
She later describes him as having a handsome nose and wise furrowed skin.
He appears before
Dalinar
in burnt orange robes. Aged, with a kindly, clean-shaven face. He stands with his head tipped back and jaw lowered as he regards the city of
Urithiru
.
His head and hands are liver-spotted and he appears frail.
Personality
Taravangian as the Wheel of Fortune
Tarot card by Tara Spruit
[2]
Taravangian is popular as a king, beloved of darkeyes as a builder of hospitals. However,
lighteyes
consider him to be less than bright. He's not an idiot, but in lighteyed politics, being only average is a disadvantage.
Jasnah
describes him as a wonderful man, worth a hundred self-proclaimed experts on courtly ways. He reminds her of her uncle, Dalinar. Earnest, sincere, concerned.
Taravangian had always been humble, and had kept to himself, so most don't know he'd been intelligent once - before his strange illness five years ago, which
Navani
is fairly certain covers an apoplexy that had permanently wounded his mental capacities.
*
Dalinar tells Navani that there's an earnestness to him, and a melancholy that he hadn't remembered.
Normally, Taravangian is bothered by the physical assistance of others to his person; he does not like being treated like an invalid. At times, however, he'll suffer the indignity as it's a lesser one than collapsing.
Dalinar thinks that he is easy to dismiss when one assumes he is slow of thought. But once one knows the truth, other mysteries begin to fit into place.
Characteristics
He resides in the royal palace, the Kharbranthian
Conclave
.
When he's smart, he's less compassionate, and when he's stupid, he's more compassionate.
On the day he created the
Diagram
- "his brightest day," he leaned more toward the
Spiritual Realm
than the Cognitive.
On those days when Taravangian is at genius capacity, the way he thinks, breathes, even moves, implicitly convey that such a day is one of intelligence, though perhaps not as brilliant as that single transcendent day when he created the Diagram.
Still, he must prove himself to not be an idiot. While he set the test up, he thinks the irony is that
Dukar
let the prohibitions instituted by Taravangian's idiot self control his true self when it finally has opportunity to emerge.
When interpreting the Diagram, Taravangian prefers to be in his bedroom, empty of furniture and nonessential persons, listening to relaxing music that's not too slow.
He shuts himself into blissful self-company, in which he arranges a
diamond
in each corner - a light to accompany that of his own spark, which shines in truth where others cannot venture - and as he finishes, a small choir of children start to sing Vorin hymns outside the room.
Beliefs
Taravangian doesn't believe in any religion, because for him they're unwieldy things, designed to fill gaps in human understanding with nonsensical explanations, allowing people to sleep well at night, granting them a false sense of comfort and control and preventing them from stretching further for true understanding.
Still, for him there is something strangely holy about the Diagram, the power of raw intelligence, the only thing man should worship.
While
Vorinism
forbids men from reading, Taravangian believes that it merely prevents an arbitrary half of the population from handling information, and that it's the stupid who should be barred from doing so.
History
Taravangian
Fan art by stinkypanda
[3]
The words of a surgeon, a master healer who had delivered Taravangian through a cut in his mother's belly were as follows:
“
"He had the cord wrapped around his neck. The queen will know the best course, but I regret to inform her that while he lives, your son may have diminished capacity. Perhaps this is one to keep on outer estates, in favor of other heirs."
”
The "diminished capacity" hadn't appeared, but the reputation had chased Taravangian from childhood, so pervasive in people's minds that not a one had seen through his recent act of stupidity, which they'd attributed to a stroke or to simple senility. Or maybe, some said, that was the way he'd always been.
He'd overcome that reputation in magnificent ways. Now he'd save the world ... or, at least the part of the world that mattered.
Taravangian granted Jasnah  access to the Palanaeum, in exchange for freeing his granddaughter who was trapped, after a recent
highstorm
had caused a cave-in within the Conclave.
He joined Jasnah and Shallan during lunch. Shallan made a drawing for him while Jasnah and the king discussed Jasnah's atheism.
When Shallan was injured, he visited her in the hospital.
Szeth
described Taravangian as no simpleton, due to his exposure to glimpses of the real person beneath the king's public face.
After Szeth's assassination of multiple
highprinces
, Jah Keved fell into civil war. Though it was of great magnitude, it ended quickly, as all the highprinces converged on the capital and quickly killed each other.
After the war was over, Taravangian visited the new king,
Valam
. On the way, he made sure his men were seen helping the survivors of war, quickly gaining support of the smallfolk.
Szeth visited him unexpectedly, telling him of the existence of another Radiant. Taravangian soothed him, told Szeth to kill Dalinar while avoiding contact with
Kaladin
, and quickly went to refer to the Diagram once Szeth had left. Here, he identified that a
bridgeman
had to be the Knight Radiant from something he wrote, and emphasized that the bridgeman and Szeth could never meet.
Afterward, he went up to where Valam was dying. After a quick conversation, Valam made his bastard son,
Redin
, bear witness to his making Taravangian the new king, then made Redin kill him.
This made Taravangian King of Jah Keved, and thus he had taken the first major step toward unifying the world.
Biography
Mastermind
Artwork by Ari Ibarra
[4]
Taravangian is the king of Kharbranth, an independent city-state, and one of the five
Vorin kingdoms
. He's not a terribly powerful king, as Kharbranth hasn't any
Shardblades
,
and the city's only exports are fish, bells, and information.
He is considered to be a weak king by many lighteyes, who say that he panders to so many other monarchs and fears war,
which is the most important
Calling
in the Vorin religion. However, he is popular among darkeyes, beloved for building and maintaining hospitals in his city,
where everyone is cared for and nobody is turned away.
He is able to maintain these hospitals by charging admittance fees into the
Palanaeum
, the largest and finest library in
Roshar
.
Publicly, he is seen as being simple-minded. In secret, however, he is a masterful plotter, with his schemes spanning across nations. Apparently, Taravangian had sought the
Old Magic
, receiving both curse and boon from the
Nightwatcher
. He wished for the capacity to save the world. As a result, his intelligence fluctuates from day to day, sometimes making him idiotic and sometimes a genius, with his compassion being inversely proportional to how smart he is.
He makes tests for himself on his smarter days and depending upon the score he achieves each morning, his guards place restrictions on what orders he can make if he scores too high or too low.
“
"We leave it to His Majesty’s mind, on a strong day, to puzzle out the meaning of why the storms might be important, and what the poem may mean by indicating that there is silence both above and below said storms."
”
–
Joshor
, Head of His Majesty’s
Silent Gatherers
, Tanatanev 1173
On a day of unparalleled intelligence, he spent 20 manic hours writing the Diagram on all the surfaces of his chamber, a guide to saving the human species, apparently deducing facts about the past
and
the future from what he already knew. He apparently guessed much about
spren
,
Voidbringers
, and the
Knights Radiant
. One of his notes indicates that he's aware of
Worldhoppers
such as
Hoid
, but isn't entirely sure of their implications and if it's possible.
Taravangian gawks like a child as he and Dalinar ride the lift within Urithiru toward the top of the tower. He leans over the side far enough that his large
Thaylen
bodyguard rests a careful hand on his shoulder, just in case. He is fascinated with the many levels and balcony.
Having later re-read the document, seeing into it in new ways, Taravangian determines that Dalinar must not be assassinated. That instead, his coalition must be supported. Then they would force him to step down, so that Taravangian can take his place at the head of the monarchs.
When Adrotagia says that they must push the
Blackthorn
so hard that he collapses, she also says that they'll need secrets to use against him, Taravangian says that will be easy. That they'll send
Malata
's spren
Spark
to spy. Further, he says Dalinar reeks of secrets. That they can can break him, and he can take his place - as the coalition will see him as nonthreatening - whereupon they'll be in a position of power to negotiate with
Odium
- who will, by laws of spren and gods, be bound by the agreement made.
Odium and Taravangian
Fan art by botanicaxu
Much, much later, Dalinar accuses Taravangian of abandoning him. Of abandoning
Thaylen City
. Taravangian replied that he assumed he was going to fall, and so positioned himself in a way that he could seize control of the coalition.
According to Taravangian, he seems to be stupid more and more often. He thinks that perhaps it is his perception. Eight days have passed since the
Battle of Thaylen Field
. He isn't certain that Dalinar will ever trust him again, but giving him some truth had been a calculated risk. For now, he is still part of the coalition.
But, Taravangian has made a deal with Odium about defending/sparing Kharbranth, essentially a pact with himself.
He now has access to what Odium could do, which is a limited ability to see the future, and its a little bit better than that of a lot of the
Shards
.
Secret Activities
Main article:
Last Words
Taravangian discovered that sometime after King
Gavilar
's excursion into the
Shattered Plains
, the
death rattles
began. Taravangian believes these to be of extreme importance.
“
"Their words might save us."
”
Secretly, he began to use his hospitals to deliberately kill people by slowly draining their blood. He keeps scholars present on-site at all times so they can record victims' death rattles.
Taravangian has no illusions about the nature of his actions, but he believes them to be necessarily evil, in his work toward the ultimate purpose of saving the world.
“
"It is better for one man to sin than for a people to be destroyed ... "
"... I am the monster who will save this world."
”
Taravangian employed Szeth to assassinate some of the most powerful and influential men on Roshar, including
Hanavanar
, previously King of Jah Keved. He believes his actions serve the purpose of bringing stability to the world, according to the prophetic series of hieroglyphs - the Diagram - that he made during a day of extraordinary genius. He orders Szeth to kill Dalinar in order to prevent the latter from uniting the
Alethi
highprinces.
At the end of Oathbringer, Taravangian makes a deal with Odium and is now currently working for the enemy.
The Diagram
Main article:
The Diagram
Relationships
Szeth
As previously stated, Taravangian employed Szeth to assassinate some of the most powerful and influential men on Roshar, including Hanavanar, previously King of Jah Keved.
Szeth later killed Taravangian, who Ascended to godhood, becoming Odium.
Jasnah
Taravangian was under the hope that Jasnah would see the world the way he does - with a very strict (even dangerous) level of utilitarianism. He thought about recruiting her to his mission, but decided against it for various reasons.
Dalinar
After ordering Szeth to kill Dalinar, and after Szeth's failure to do so, Taravangian believes that it would have been so much better for Dalinar to have died by the assassin's blade.
“
"For now, we must leave him to the enemy's desires, and that will not be as kind as a quick death."
”
–Taravangian to Adrotagia
Further, Taravangian believes that Dalinar would be the first to tell another that when one's opponent is getting back up, one must act quickly to crush his knees. He says that then, one's opponent will bow, and present his skull.
Adrotagia
Main article:
Adrotagia
Taravangian (in one of his duller moments) thinks she looks so old now. He wonders whether she feels as he did, shocked every time she looks in the mirror, wondering where the days have gone. When they'd been young, they'd wanted to conquer the world. Or save it.
Dukar
Main article:
Dukar
Joshor
Main article:
Joshor
Mrall
Main article:
Mrall
Malata
Main article:
Malata
F'en Rnamdi
After having seen the suffering of the people of Thaylen City in the wake of the passage of the
Everstorm
, Taravangian offers Queen Fen the use of his surgeons to ease that suffering. She tells him that his help would be appreciated.
Wit
Taravangian stole a few of Wit's Breaths, and they had memories attached to them.
Notes
Taravangian visited the Nightwatcher sometime after Gavilar's assassination.
Conversely, according to
Brandon
, Taravangian met with Cultivation.
*
In Alethkar, this man would never have been able to hold a throne after the apoplexy struck him. An unscrupulous family would've removed him by assassination. In other families, someone would've challenged him for his throne. He'd have been forced to fight or abdicate.
Utilitarianism was definitely the inspiration or part of the inspiration for Taravangian.
Again according to Brandon, though a lot of deep into the
cosmere
people are interested in the original
Shards
and getting their stories, for the average reader Taravangian is a much more identifiable villain. He's been building him from The Way of Kings to be not just really scary, but a philosophical opposite to Dalinar.
“
"The cost is Odium stops being the evil you don't know. The evil you don't know is a very powerful force in fantasy literature. The evil you do know does different things. And I lose that evil you don't know though you still have a bit of it, because the power of Odium - the Shard itself - I wouldn't say has volition completely, but it's still there and its a thing. It is constrained by Taravangian and directed by Taravangian, but it's the rage of a deity separated from its morals should be a scary thing. In the hands of someone who is essentially a fallible mortal, should be an even more scary thing."
”
–Brandon Sanderson
Further, the major cost is that Odium is a
better
ancient unknowable evil. Ancient thing, very dangerous, very strange, very powerful.
Q&A with Brandon
Q. Is there a pattern to Taravangian's stupid/smart cycle, or is it actually random?
A. His aides are convinced that it is random, but if you plotted it out, it's really a distribution curve that is only made to look random.
Q. When he made the Diagram he said it was "his brightest day." Would you say that he was more towards the
Cognitive Realm
or more towards the Spiritual Realm?
A. Spiritual.

Teft's parents
were members of the Envisagers. They believed in the

(the Ideals), the

, the

, and especially old
Vorinism
.

PAGE: Teleb
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/Teleb
======================================================================

Teleb
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Alethi
Nationality
Alethi
Gender
Male
Status
Dead
Abilities
Shardbearer
Social Information
Title(s)
Highlord
Family
Kalami
(wife)
Occupation
Soldier
Appears in
The Way of Kings
,
Words of Radiance
,
Oathbringer
Teleb
was a
lighteyed
officer and Shardbearer in
Dalinar
's
warcamp
on
Roshar
. He was the highlord on duty who reported a
chasmfiend
attack to Dalinar.
He had suggested using lighter, more portable
bridges
to get across early
chasms
on the
Shattered Plains
, then resort to siege bridges only for the final assault.
He was also the highlord who escorted the prisoner,
Rlain
, into Dalinar's presence once Rlain had turned himself over to the Alethi. (Teleb later released Rlain, at Dalinar's instruction, into the custody of
Bridge Four
.
)
Teleb was killed during the
battle
of
Narak
.
His
Shardblade
was recovered by Captain
Khal
.
Appearance
Teleb was described as being straight-backed and he had light green eyes. He had long hair and a blue tattoo on his cheek, which marked him as an "Oldblood Shardbearer."
Personality
He was described as being as literal as rocks.
According to Dalinar, he was usually unflappable.
Characteristics
Teleb liked to put on a good show of military preparedness.
He wore
Adolin
's
Plate
, which was
Evi
's Plate.
History
Teleb was the officer who reported to Dalinar that a seemingly simple supply caravan, included someone in
Shardplate
. Further, that it was from a
highprince
.
*
Dalinar then tells Teleb that he has command of their forces and that
Tanalan
's
heir
in
Rathalas
is going to stand down, but that this isn't a surrender. He says that in this manner, the heir will save face and preserve his title.
Teleb obeys, asking no questions.
When Dalinar later asks Teleb if he sent the same scouts who first spied on the caravan, and reported seeing a Shardbearer leading it, to look for him following a landslide that left him buried, Teleb confirms it. Dalinar then tells him that these men are traitors and that they're working with the heir.
Teleb barks out orders for the scouts to be imprisoned.
Shortly thereafter, Teleb and
Sadeas
joined the fight on the walls of the Rift and routed the last of the defenders, sending them down from the walls toward the chasm of the city itself.
After the initial assault, Teleb tells Dalinar that there are tens of thousands of people in the city. In response, Dalinar tells Teleb that the kingdom must know the price of rebellion.
When Teleb then asks Dalinar, "Obey or die?," Dalinar tells him that it's the same deal he offered Teleb, and that he was smart enough to take it.
After Dalinar's soldiers began dousing the city with oil and set it afire, Teleb remained solemn.
Notes
*
That highprince was suspected to be Sadeas, but such was in fact a lie perpetuated by the heir to deter Dalinar..

PAGE: Temoo
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/Temoo
======================================================================

Temoo
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Selan
Nationality
Duladelan
Gender
Male
Status
Alive
Abilities
Worldhopper
,
AonDor
Physical Description
Hair Color
Bald
Eye Color
Brown
Social Information
Aliases
Temoo, Grump
Occupation
Farmer, Elantrian,
The Seventeenth Shard
(affiliated)
Appears in
Elantris
,
The Way of Kings
Temoo
is one of three men who traveled to the
Purelake
to ask
Ishikk
's help in locating
Hoid
. He gives the name Temoo, but has been nicknamed "Grump" by Ishikk.
His traveling companions are
Vao
and
Thinker
, yet their allegiances and motivations for their search remain unknown.
His final question and comment after their departure:
“
Where are you Roamer? What a fool's quest this is.
”
(One can infer that the foreigners have nicknamed Hoid "Roamer" due to his activities involving Worldhopping.)
He has been identified as Galladon from Duladel, a reference to Elantris.
Appearance
Temoo is described as dark-skinned
- not unlike a
Makabaki
, most of whom were small and fine-boned - yet with thick limbs and a completely bald head.
Personality
There is something innately likeable about Galladon, something that Raoden (of Elantris) has expressed. Galladon takes his time. Everything he does is performed with an air of utter precision. However, he is pessimistic, an eternal skeptic. He sees a taint on everything, like the sludge of Elantris. He was almost glad when the Shaod took him.
He seems a well of knowledge at times, but in other areas he is completely ignorant - and totally unconcerned by that fact. He takes everything in stride, but complains about it at the same time.
Biography
Galladon's father was an Elantrian. He lived with him until he was old enough to move away. He always thought it was wrong for a Dula to live in Arelon, away from his people and his family. Galladon left his father a poor, lonely god in a divine city, who died about a year after he left.
He identified himself as a farmer and an orchard keeper in his life prior to that of an Elantrian. He stated therein that he sold his fields and bought an apple farm because it would be easier to farm apples than wheat. However, he didn't fit in Duladel, no matter how much he enjoyed his farming.
Belief System
Galladon expressed that he'd initially been taught that one took one's time. Everything he did was performed with an air of utter precision. He also expressed that he gained knowledge from his conversations with others. Additionally, he has gained knowledge from books which he has read.
He also believed that he deserved the city of Elantris, and that it deserved him. He guessed that the Dor had decided to give him the Elantrian curse.
Further, Galladon believes that it takes a fine sense of pessimism to brood with any sort of respectability.

Teshav Khal
is a scribe and the wife of one of
Dalinar
's officers, General Khal.
Apparently, she always needs something to worry about. For the most part, though, Dalinar trusts her.
She tells Dalinar that
Adolin
had asked her to report the progress of the saddle girth investigation. She says that Adolin was speaking with leatherworkers at this very moment, but so far, there was very little to say. That nobody witnessed anyone interfering with the saddle or the king's horse. Further, she says that their spies have said that there are no whispers of anyone in the other
warcamps
bragging, and no one in their own camp has suddenly received large sums of money, so far as they'd discovered.
Appearance
Teshav has streaks of blonde in her otherwise black Alethi hair, which she wears up in an intricate weave. She has violet eyes, and a pinched face with a concerned expression.
Characteristics
She is one of Alethkar's finest political minds.

Brightlord

======================================================================
PAGE: The Archer
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/The_Archer
======================================================================

The Archer
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Alethi
Nationality
Alethi
Gender
Male
Status
Alive
Occupation
Soldier
Appears in
Oathbringer
The
archer
(of thirty-four years ago) who fires a
black blur
- which turns out to be an arrow with a long, thick shaft - that hits
Dalinar
in his right shoulder with the force of a roundhouse kick, straight through his chain mail, is immediately sought out by Dalinar, who is impressed with man's prowess at having made his shot from over three hundred yards.
The next arrow fired by the archer lands a mere foot from Dalinar, who had jumped out of the way.
Yet another arrow is dodged by Dalinar as he seizes the reins of his
horse
.
Once Dalinar bursts on top of the ridge where the archer is located, still another arrow slams into his
left
breast, going straight through the breastplate near the shoulder, nearly throwing him from the saddle.
The archer, still a distant figure to Dalinar, stands upon a rocky knob and launches another arrow. And another. Dalinar is impressed with just how quick he is.
Finally, the archer grows alarmed, and leaps from his perch to flee. Dalinar sends him sprawling with a kick to his back.
Seizing the archer, Dalinar responds ...
“
"You waited until my helm was off,"
Dalinar demanded.
"You are an assassin. You were set here
specifically
to kill me."
”
The archer winces, then nods, and Dalinar reacts with enthusiasm, asking the archer to make the shot again, but on a corpse down below, similarly three to four hundred yards distant. So, having surveyed the area with a spyglass off his belt, the archer selects a corpse, then fires a single, black-fletched arrow, which flows true, sticking into the chosen corpse.
The bow with which he does so is made of a sleek black wood Dalinar doesn't recognize.
Dalinar then welcomes the man to his
elites
, yet the man protests, indicating that he'd just tried to kill Dalinar, to which Dalinar replies ...
“
"Yes, from a distance. Which shows remarkably good judgment. I can make use of someone with your skills."
”
–Dalinar to the archer
The archer responds that they're enemies, yet Dalinar replies that they're all allies now, his forces having defeated their opponent.
Further, Dalinar tells him that he can respect it if the archer would rather be killed than join his forces. Alternatively, he offers the archer his price to join. When the archer names the life of his
Brightlord
Yezriar
, Dalinar looks to
Thakka
to confirm that Yezriar is the fellow he just killed ... which the Captain of his elites confirms.
The archer asks Dalinar if he couldn't have instead captured his lord, to which Dalinar replies in the negative.  Still, Dalinar asks the archer to join him, stating that his soldiers won't pillage the town in exchange for his doing so.
Later, the sullen archer is seen by Dalinar to be on a horse with one of the other men.
When Dalinar heard a muttered curse from his new archer as he passed the heads of the former highprince and his officers up on spears, he determined that he'd have to talk to the man, reinforce that in striking at Dalinar earlier, he'd shot an arrow at an enemy. That was to be respected. If he tried something against Dalinar or
Sadeas
now, it would be different.
Appearance
The archer is a man in his twenties who wears rugged clothing, and has arms and shoulders that look like they could have lifted a
chull
.
He has a blue tattoo on his cheek.

Knights Radiant
Appears in
The Way of Kings
(mentioned),
Words of Radiance
(mentioned)
Oathbringer
The Blackthorn
is the moniker by which Dalinar has been known since his younger years while fighting for and under the command of his brother
Gavilar
, then king of Alethkar. His prowess in battle earned him the nickname and a reputation amongst the common folk as being one of the greatest warriors and generals who ever lived. He is considered to be a genius of the battlefield, a fearsome warlord, and a living legend.
When confronted by his son,
Adolin
, who suggests his father pick someone who is naming him a coward, challenge that individual, and remind everyone what a mistake it is to insult the Blackthorn, his father tells him that he cannot because the
Alethi Codes of War
forbid it for one of his stature.
Roion
is one such who said that there were others who had said that the Blackthorn had lost his sting.
But, in his own mind, the Blackthorn was a natural force, never to be halted. He was death itself. Still, even in acknowledging this he feels a sudden stab of powerful revulsion, a sickness so strong that it makes him gasp ... and the
Thrill
vanishes from him.
Character
“
"I'm like an animal, Gavilar. Did you hear about the bar fight? Storms. I can't be trusted around people."
”
–Dalinar to Gavilar
Characteristics
If anyone was a threat to the throne, it was the Blackthorn - particularly with the respect he'd gained from the kingdom's generals.
History
After a tavern brawl twenty-three years ago, one of the brawlers he fought would never walk again. Another had to have his arm removed. A third babbled like a child because his brain didn't work anymore.
Havar
told Dalinar that when facing the Blackthorn, that was as good as one could expect.
After being rejected in a fight by a
wrestler
for which he'd been so ready, he felt the shame of losing control, but none of the satisfaction of actually getting to fight. He'd surrendered to the temptation, and then had the pleasure stolen from him.
References

PAGE: The Convict
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/The_Convict
======================================================================

The Convict
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Reshi
Nationality
Unknown
Gender
Male
Status
Unknown
Appears in
Oathbringer
The convict
is a man who hides in a tree at the
Purelake
. Lacking the sense to stay hidden there, he leaps for
Szeth
, perhaps tempted by the prospect of obtaining a fine weapon (i.e.,
Nightblood
).
Szeth sidesteps and escapes the slashes of the convict's improvised dagger, but is forced toward the water.
The criminal then rushes him. Szeth catches the the convict's hand with the dagger, twisting to use the man's own momentum to send him stumbling into the lake.
When he rushes Szeth again, Szeth catches the man by the wrist once more and spins him around, the water splashing. The man drops his knife, which Szeth plucks from the water. He dodges the man's grapple, and in a moment has one arm around the convict's neck. Szeth raises the knife and presses the blade against the man's chest, drawing blood.
Szeth lowers the knife, but this gives the man an opening to twist and pull them both down into the Purelake. The criminal lands on top and forces Szeth under the surface, slamming his hand against the stony bottom and making him drop the knife.
When Szeth throws off the criminal, Nightblood gets caught between the stones of the lake bottom, causing him to jerk back to the water. He rips himself free, breaking the sword's harness strap, and the weapon sinks into the water. The man glances at the submerged sword, his eyes glaze, then he grins wickedly, droping his knife, and dives for the sword. The convict comes up looking gleeful, holding the weapon.
Szeth punches him across the face, grabs his sheathed sword, ripping it from the weaker man's hands, then swings it at his enemy. The weapon strikes the convict's back and he splashes down into the lake and falls still. Szeth shakes himself, then kneels and pulls the man up by his matted hair. The convict gasps, but his body doesn't move. Not dead, but paralyzed.
When Szeth asks him whether someone worked with him in his escape, the convict replies that it was
Vun Makak
.
When Szeth asks him how he got out, the convict tells him that it was
Nu Ralik
. That he just wanted to see the sun again.
When Szeth leaves the man to drown, he tells Nightblood that it was better than leaving him to a
greatshell
, which happens there in that kingdom.
Appearance
His sorry appearance is ragged. He has matted, shaggy hair. His skin bears many lesions. According to Szeth, the man is so filthy that beggars and street urchins would find him distasteful company. He is also scrawny and weak.
References

PAGE: The Crier
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/The_Crier
======================================================================

The Crier
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Veden
/
Unkalaki
Nationality
Unknown
Gender
Female
Status
Unknown
Occupation
Crier
Appears in
Words of Radiance
The
crier
carried a writ with the king's words on it - bound between two cloth-covered boards - though she had the words of the writ memorized. To her annoyance,
Kaladin
alone had made her repeat the proclamation three times ... then yet again.
Idly, Kaladin thought that in any other battalion, its leader would be of a high enough social class to outrank her.
There would be dozens of women like her moving through the
warcamp
to read, and sometimes explain,
Dalinar
's words.
Her message was as follows:
“
"Under the authority of the king,"
she said,
"Dalinar Kholin, Highprince of War, hereby orders changes to the manner of collection and distribution of gemhearts on the Shattered Plains. Henceforth, each gemheart will be collected in turn by two highprinces working in tandem. The spoils become the property of the king, who will determine - based on the effectiveness of the parties involved and their alacrity to obey - their share.
"A prescribed rotation will detail which highprinces and armies are responsible for hunting gemhearts, and in what order. The pairings will not always be the same, and will be judged based on strategic compatibility. It is expected that by the Codes we all hold dear, the men and women of these armies will welcome this renewed focus on victory and, at long last, vengeance."
”
Having delivered it, she nodded to Kaladin, then moved off toward the next battalion square.
Appearance
She was a plump,
lighteyed
young woman with strands of red hair mixed in her black, bespeaking Veden and/or Horneater heritage.
She cocked a long black eyebrow toward Kaladin that he was pretty sure had been painted on with makeup.

======================================================================
PAGE: The Girl Who Looked Up
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/The_Girl_Who_Looked_Up
======================================================================

The Girl Who Looked Up
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Unknown
Nationality
Unknown
Gender
Female
Status
Dead
Physical Description
Hair Color
White
Appears in
Oathbringer
(mentioned)
The Girl Who Looked Up
, according to
Shallan
, was a girl before storms, before memories, and before legends.
Again, according to Shallan, the girl played and danced, as girls do to this day. Further, Shallan says that most things were the same then as they are today. Except for one big difference. The wall.
In those days, this wall had kept out the storms, Shallan states. Further, that it had existed for so long, nobody knew how it had been built. This didn't bother those people in that time.
Wit
reveals to Shallan that in those days, there was no light. Further, that even without light, people still had to live. So they lived in the darkness, farmed in the darkness, ate in the darkness. The people stumbled about in their village, feeling their way to different activities, barely able to see by the starlight.
Still, the girl looked up at the wall and asked some of those people why there was a wall, what bad things was it to keep out, and what was it to protect them from. She was told that if she went beyond it she would die.
However, the girl realizes that the wall wasn't to keep something in, but to keep her and her people out.
Wit reiterates this, telling Shallan that the wall kept people out because beyond the wall was God's Light.
The girl then climbs a tree, peeks out the top, her scarf streaming behind her, and asks a boy sleeping lazily in the nook of a branch why there's a wall. When the boy asks her what wall, the girl points toward the wall. The boy replies that that's not a wall. That it's just the way the sky is over there. The girl then says that it's a wall, a giant wall. The boy then says that it must be there for a purpose and that if she goes beyond it, she'll probably die.
Shallan continues by saying that the answers to the girl's questions didn't satisfy the girl. The girl reasoned to herself, if the wall kept evil things out, then the space on her side of it should be safe.
Even so, the girl sneaked from her home one night with a bundle of supplies. She walked toward the wall, and indeed the land was safe. But it was also dark. Always in the shadow of the wall. No sunlight, ever, directly reached the people.
The girl traveled far, but no predators hunted her, and no storms assaulted her. The only wind was the pleasant one that played with her scarf, and the only creatures she saw were the
cremlings
that clicked at her as she walked.
At long last, the girl stood before the wall, which was truly expansive, running as far as she could see in either direction.
The girl then climbed. Though there were outcroppings in the wall, she'd climbed the highest trees all through her youth. She could do this.
The climb took days. At night, the girl would tie herself a hammock out of her scarf and sleep there. She picked out her village at one point, remarking on how small it seemed, now that she was high.
As she neared the top, she finally began to fear what she would find on the other side. Unfortunately, this fear didn't stop her. She was young, and questions bothered her more than fear. So it was that she finally struggled to the very top and stood to see the other side. The hidden side.
The girl stood triumphantly on top of the wall, her scarf and white hair streaming out behind her in a sudden wind.
On that side of the wall, Shallan says, the girl saw steps. The back side of the wall was crisscrossed with enormous sets of steps leading down to the ground, so distant.
Yet again, according to Shallan, the girl stared at those steps, and suddenly (to Shallan) the gruesome statues on her own side of the wall within the tower of
Urithiru
made sense. The spears. The way it cast everything into shadow. The wall did indeed hide something evil, something frightening. It was the people, like the girl and her village.
The girl then went down the steps, and saw a perfect society lit by
Stormlight
. She stole some and brought it back.
She sneaked up to the Light and brought it back to the land of shadows.
The storms then came as a punishment, tearing down the wall.
The people suffered, but each storm brought light renewed, for it could never be put back, now that it had been taken. And people, for all their hardship, would never choose to go back. Not now that they could see.
The Girl Who Looked Up
Fan art by Bryan Kolb
[1]
Appearance
She wore a long scarf to blow in the wind and had white hair.
Notes
The Girl Who Looked Up is a story that Shallan reveals to
Pattern
through her drawings and storytelling while they are both on a theater stage within the tower of Urithiru.
When Pattern asks her whether this was how the storms first began, she tells him of course not. That the story is a lie. That it doesn't mean anything.
He then asks her why she's crying, but she then wipes her eyes and turns away from the empty stage, needing to get back to the markets.
The Girl Who Looked Up is also a story that Wit recalls for Shallan, for which he encourages her to create illusions to accompany it.
When asked if he's ever thought about doing children's books,
Brandon
replied that they've been thinking of doing a picture book of The Girl Who Looked Up.
When asked whether the girl's hair was long or short, Brandon replied that it was long.
When
Peter
was asked on 9/30/20 whether the story of The Girl Who Looked Up was the first instance in which the people from
Ashyn
, then relocated to
Roshar
, finally broke free of
Shinovar
to populate all of Roshar, he replied that all he could say was RAFO.
[citation needed]
Q&A with Brandon
Q. So the white hair. Did she have white hair or did Shallan just imagine that she had white hair?
A. That is another RAFO.
Q. There are a lot of RAFO's about
The Girl Who Looked Up
.
A. There are a lot of RAFO's.
Q. So, the girl who looked up, could her hair change like the royal locks?
A. RAFO.
References

PAGE: The Little Girl
CATEGORIES: Characters

The Little Girl
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Alethi
Nationality
Alethi
Gender
Female
Status
Alive
Family
Kheni
(adoptive mother),
Cob
(adoptive father)
Appears in
Oathbringer
The little girl
is about four years old. She refuses to leave her mama (deceased) when
Wit
tries to draw her out from a pile of rubble in
Kholinar
with a doll he's made for her from the remnants of a
spren
costume.
When he smiles at her, she tells him his teeth look funny. When he asks her if she knows the answer to a question he's asked that she couldn't hear, she tells him that he's weird.
When she asks whether the doll is for her, he tells her that he can't take the doll with him. That someone needs to care for her. That she's afraid of the darkness. That the little girl would need to keep her in the light.
When she still won't leave her mama, Wit raises the doll to his lips, then whispers a choice set of words. When he sets the doll down, it starts to walk on its own. A soft gasp sounds inside the shadows. The doll toddles toward the street and the little girl emerges from the shadows and runs to get it.
She hugs the patchwork creation, and Wit picks her up, turning away from the broken building - and the bones of a leg sticking from the rubble. He then carries the little girl back to the square, quietly pushes the empty cradle next to Kheni away from her, and kneels before her.
He tells Kheni that he has to leave the city and someone needs to take care of the little girl. Kheni then holds out her arms and Wit puts the child into them. Kheni's husband takes him by the arm, smiling.
Wit then touches the doll in the child's hands and tells it to forget what he told it before; he tells it to instead, take care of her.
Appearance
She has a dirty face and a grimy hand.
References

PAGE: The Nobleman Jailer

The Nobleman Jailer
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Unknown
Nationality
Unknown
Gender
Male
Status
Dead
Occupation
Jailer
Appears in
Oathbringer
The nobleman jailer
took part in a prison economy, whereby food went to those in power while others were starved.
He failed spectacularly trying to make small talk with Master
Ki
, and accosted
Szeth
for questioning him with regard to the man's prison guard practices.
When Szeth told him that the prisoners entrusted to him were mistreated, locked in darkness, not given enough food, the nobleman responded that they were criminals. Murderers.
When Szeth asked him what happened to the money he was sent to administrate the prison (because it certainly hadn't gone toward proper security), the nobleman responded that he didn't have to listen to that which Szeth had said.
When Szeth asked Master Ki whether she had a writ of execution for the man, she told him that it was the first that the
Skybreakers
had obtained.
Szeth then slammed
Nightblood
through the chest of the blubbering nobleman, killing him, and told Master Ki that he'd killed the worst of the criminals he and his fellow
squires
had been tasked to kill or capture. She commented that he'd done well.
According to Master Ki, when asked by Szeth just why the man hadn't been dealt with earlier, despite that he'd been investigated by the Skybreakers with regard to just how he was using his funds, she responded that the nobleman had not yet broken a law. Further, that his duty was to imprison convicts, which he'd done. She told Szeth that the man had been allowed to judge if his methods were satisfactory or not. However, only once he'd failed, and his charges had escaped (i.e., the prisoners that Szeth and his fellow squires had pursued and either killed or captured, like the
convict
whom Szeth killed), could she and her fellows mete out justice.
Appearance
He was a beefy man.

Nale

shopkeeper

Much later,
Ghenna
reports that, with regard to the
Everstorm
, the prince is reportedly preparing a diktat of emergency for the entire country.
Still later, a guard flings open a door to a room in the
Grand Indicium
in which are
Lift
and
Wyndle
to announce the following:
“
"Status Five emergency diktat from the prince, to be disseminated through the nation immediately. State of emergency in the city."
”
By the prince's orders, given the warnings from
Dalinar
,
parshmen
are gathered and turned out of the city of
Yeddaw
.
Much later,
Szeth
informs Lift that Nale has procured an injunction from the prince, forbidding any use of
Surgebinding
in the country, save that specifically authorized.

The Shopkeeper

Tashikki

Appears in

,

The shopkeeper
is a market street vendor in the city of
Yeddaw
. His wares are fruits.
When
Tiqqa
swipes a whole basket of fruit from him, he shouts for her to stop.
Soon afterward, he thanks
Darkness
for apprehending the thief, calling him "officer."

Shardblade
, the shopkeeper screams, dropping the basket of fruit she'd stolen.

With the fruit spilled at his feet, the shopkeeper stares at Tiqqa's corpse,
angerspren
boiling up around him. He demands of Darkness whether what he had done was necessary, stating that it was barbaric.

PAGE: The Soulcaster
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/The_Soulcaster
======================================================================

The Soulcaster
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Alethi
Nationality
Alethi
Gender
Unknown
Status
Unknown
Occupation
Ardent
Appears in
Oathbringer
The Soulcaster
at
Gavilar
's
rite
of interment might have been male, might have been female. It was hard to determine, as they wore a hood up over their face. The skin beneath was colored like granite, cracked and chipped, and seemed to glow from within. The Soulcaster regarded the corpse, head cocked, as if surprised to find a body there in royal catacombs of
Kholinar
. They ran their fingers along Gavilar's jaw, then brushed the hair off his forehead.
They whispered to the corpse, then tapped a stone that had replaced one of the king's eyes. Then, light emerged as the Soulcaster drew their hand from their pocket, revealing a set of
gemstones
bound into a
fabrial
.
The Soulcaster touched Gavilar on the forehead, and the transformation happened instantly. One moment Gavilar was there. The next he had become a statue.
The Soulcaster slipped a glove onto their hand while other ardents hurried to remove the wires that had held Gavilar's body in position. They used levers to tip him carefully forward until he was standing, holding a sword with point toward the ground, his other hand outstretched. He stared toward eternity, crown on his head, the curls of his beard and hair preserved delicately in the stone. A powerful pose; the mortuary sculptors had done a fantastic job.

The Taker of Secrets
The Taker of Secrets
Drawing by Miranda Meeks
[1]

is the alias by which Sja-anat is known.
She is one of the Unmade, a mysterious group of
odious
spren
on
Roshar
.
She speaks to
Shallan
shortly after Shallan enters the
revel
atop the
Oathgate
platform in
Kholinar
.
According to Shallan,
Dalinar
's
visions
mention her.
Description
In a page from the volume
Mythica
, she is described as follows:
“
Sja-anat, creator and corrupter - unique among the Unmade.
Creator:
Her twisted creations are her beloved children.
Her admiration of the spren of our world inspires her.
Corruptor:
She seeks the Children of Honor and the Children of Cultivation.
With one touch, she corrupts.
”
Notes
Brandon
has confirmed that the Unmade are
Splinters
of Odium.
Odium splintered himself and created the Unmade to be his influence on Roshar. It is through the Unmade that Odium may be able to influence people in how they think or act.

The Tattoist
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Alethi
Nationality
Alethi
Gender
Female
Status
Unknown
Occupation
Artist
Appears in
Words of Radiance
The tattooist
is a calm, sturdy darkeyed woman.
The rest of
Bridge Four
stands around
Hobber
in the tent, watching in silence as the tattooist works carefully on his forehead, covering up the scar of his
slave
's brand with the
glyphs
Kaladin
had provided.
Once the process is finished, the tattooist provides a mirror for Hobber to look into. When he indicates that it's not enough, she settles down on her stool again and begins adding the Bridge Four glyphs to Hobber's forehead, tucked below the freedom glyph now there. She spends the process explaining - again - how the tattoo will be sore for days and how Hobber will need to care for it.
She then applies the same glyphs to
Skar
's,
Rock
's,
Beld
's, and
Teft
's foreheads, but when
Shen
steps forward, she says that she can't tattoo him because he's property.
Teft quickly interjects that Shen has been freed like the rest of them, yet Hobber goes one step further, telling her to give him the tattoo, or she won't see a
sphere
in payment.
The tattoo artist finally sighs and gives in. She pulls her stool over and begins working on Shen's forehead, grumbling that they won't even be able to see it, though
Sigzil
's skin is nearly as dark as that of Shen, and the tattoo had shown up fine on him.
Appearance
She looks like she could have lifted a bridge all on her own.

PAGE: Thinker
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/Thinker
======================================================================

Thinker
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Scadrian
Nationality
Unknown
Gender
Male
Status
Alive
Abilities
Worldhopper
Social Information
Aliases
Demoux
Family
Aslydin
(love interest)
Occupation
The Seventeenth Shard
(affiliated)
Appears in
Mistborn
,
The Way of Kings
Thinker
is one of the three men who travel the
Purelake
in search of
Hoid
. He has introduced himself with a different name, but
Ishikk
has nicknamed him Thinker (correctly), assuming the name provided him is false. His traveling companions give their names as
Temoo
and
Vao
.
Characteristics
Thinker was described by Ishikk as thoughtful, with
Alethi
-like light tan skin, but wrongly shaped eyes and accent. For these, Ishikk decided he was certainly not Alethi. He spoke the
Selay
language worse than his two companions, and usually stayed quiet.
As Demoux, he viewed
Kelsier
as a god and was a preacher in the
Church of the Survivor
.
Given Demoux's religious fervor, there's still a reason he's not a
Ghostblood
, though
Brandon
won't yet go into that.
Notes
The man called Thinker is a confirmed Worldhopper, initially introduced in
Brandon
's Mistborn series as Demoux.
Demoux is the only worldhopper that readers have seen who inherently ages.
Q&A with Brandon
Q. Did Demoux achieve immortality by manipulating his
Connection
age?
A. Demoux uses the same method as most of the Seventeenth Shard. That method slows aging by a lot, but doesn't stop it completely.

gemstones
. He dresses in simple garments that were tailored around the chitinous armor plates on his arms, legs and chest. He also wore a long coat, but he wouldn't take that to battle.

PAGE: Timbre
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/Timbre
======================================================================

Timbre
Biographical information
Race
Spren
Ethnicity
Reacher
Nationality
Shadesmar
Gender
Female
Status
Alive
Abilities
Nahel bonding
, affording
Surgebinding
Social Information
Title(s)
Lightspren
Family
Ico
(father),
Grandfather
Occupation
Willshaperspren
Appears in
Words of Radiance
(mentioned),
Oathbringer
,
Rhythm of War
Timbre
is a lightspren originally meant for -  and who began bonding with -
Eshonai
,
yet later was encountered by
Venli
after Venli had discovered Eshonai's corpse.
After experiencing the transformation of her once-mate
Demid
, plus that of
Melu
,
Altoki
, and
Mrun
, Venli again discovers the little spren that looks like a ball of light, which seems agitated, as it scoots closer to her. She instantly knows something - an instinctive truth, that if the
Fused
standing nearby see this spren, they'll destroy it. She slaps her hand down over the spren as the creature (i.e.,
Hariel
) wearing
Demid
's body turns toward her. She cups the little spren against the stone close to her.
Timbre is now in the process of bonding with Venli, despite that Venli was initially reluctant to do so, thinking that the better sister with whom to bond had died. Still, Venli starts interacting with Timbre more after she learns that she's able to communicate with the spren through
listener
rhythms
. Venli names her Timbre as a result.
Timbre is kept by Venli in a pouch, yet she peeks around, looking in corners and shadowed places each time Venli lets her out.
“
Timbre floated over to her, then hovered by the window, as if to sneak out and look around outside.
"No,"
Venli said.
Timbre pulsed to Resolve, then inched forward in the air.
"Stay inside,"
Venli said to Command.
"They're watching for spren like you. Descriptions of your kind, and others, have been spread all through the city."
The little spren backed away, pulsing to Annoyance, before settling in the air beside Venli.
”
Appearance
According to Eshonai, it danced around her head and shed rings of light from its cometlike form.
Timbre is described as looking like a small ball of white fire that gives off little rings of light and trails a streak behind her like a comet.
History
As Eshonai was experiencing a true
form of power
that had not been seen in centuries (i.e.,
stormform
), a form given of the gods, she waved her hand as she climbed the central spire of
Narak
, trying to shoo away the tiny spren.
She thought it to be a horrid thing and wondered why it would not leave her alone.
Eshonai first met Timbre during a
highstorm
. She appeared near a
chasmfiend
.
When Venli later witnesses
thunderclasts
awakening in the
Physical Realm
, as the
Fused
kneel and bow their heads toward
Odium
, she too kneels quickly. Timbre pulses to Anxiety, and Venli puts her hand on the pouch in which Timbre is held, squeezing it, telling the spren to be quiet. She tells Timbre that they cannot fight him.
Family
Ico remarked upon his daughter, Timbre, abandoning her employment at the moneychangers' in
Celebrant
"to chase foolish dreams."
Later, while in conversation with Timbre, Venli learned that Timbre's grandfather was a spren killed by the
Recreance
, just like Ico's father (i.e., Timbre's other grandfather?) had been.
The question remains as to whether this person is Timbre's grandfather on her father's side, or on the side of her mother.
Abilities
Timbre has exhibited the ability to reside within Venli's
gemheart
and to capture other spren residing therein as well.
Doing so allows Venli to enter and exit forms of power granted by
Voidspren
at will.
It is unclear as yet by just what means Timbre does so.
Theory
It is likely that Timbre is a Willshaperspren because Venli seems to be on her way to becoming a Willshaper, given that after witnessing the confrontation between
Dalinar
and
Odium
at the
Battle of Thaylen Field
, Venli states the
First Ideal
of the
Knights Radiant
and begins the process of becoming one such.

PAGE: Tiqqa
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/Tiqqa
======================================================================

Tiqqa
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Tashikki
Nationality
Tashikki
Gender
Female
Status
Dead
Occupation
Thief
Appears in
Arcanum Unbounded
,
Edgedancer
Tiqqa
was an opportunist, a girl a few years
Lift
's senior, who swiped a whole basket of fruit from a
shopkeeper
in
Yeddaw
.
She bolted the moment she was spotted. She leaned down, clinging to the basket, then sprinted deftly through the crowd.
Nevertheless,
Darkness
snatched her from the crowd. She struggled, battering against him, though he remained stiff and didn't seem to notice or mind the attack. She grabbed a piece of fruit from the basket and threw it at him, bouncing it off his chest with a splat.
She then cringed before him, seemed to panic, reached to her belt, yanked out her knife and brandished it. She tried a desperate swing at his arm, but he easily slapped the weapon away with his empty hand.
Several street urchins bet on on how long it would be before Tiqqa escaped "this time."
As if in response to this, Darkness summoned his
Shardblade
and rammed it through the struggling girl's chest. It sank up to its hilt as he pulled her onto it, and she gasped, eyes going wide - then shriveling and burning out, twin trails of smoke creeping toward the sky.
Once Darkness left the scene,
Lift
forced her way to the fallen girl and attempted to reincorporate her, breathing out her "awesomeness", imparting it to the dead young woman.
For a moment, Lift saw a luminescence in the shape of a figure, vibrating around the corpse, quivering. Then, it puffed away, Tiqqa's body on the ground, her eyes burned.
Her body didn't even bleed.
“
"Her name was Tiqqa."
Lift thought.
"I will remember you, Tiqqa. Because few others will."
”

PAGE: Toh
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/Toh
======================================================================

Toh
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Riran
Nationality
Riran
Gender
Male
Status
Unknown
Social Information
Title(s)
Brightlord
Family
Evi
(sister)
Appears in
Oathbringer
Toh
is the brother of Evi who negotiates the terms of her betrothal to
Dalinar
, with regard to her transference of the
Shardplate
they possess in exchange for both their protection from their own people.
Still, despite that he does so, he and she both delay a painfully long time to even agree to the betrothal.
Yet, Toh is responsible for delivering to Dalinar the news that Evi had gone into labor with their first child.
That said, Dalinar has since not seen Toh in a decade. Toh went to
Herdaz
and reportedly liked it quite well, living on the coast, protected by
Alethi
bodyguards.
Appearance
He is tall, has blonde hair and very pale skin. There is a certain sense of flimsiness to him with his slender build.
Though he eventually wears Alethi clothing, it fits him poorly. He is too spindly.
He has an overcautious gait and a wide-eyed innocence about him.
Personality
According to
Gavilar
, Toh wonders if he and Dalinar, and the Alethi military, are strong enough to protect him.
Character
Gavilar thought that Toh was innocent and wide-eyed, but still thought him to be genuine.
Characteristics
Again, according to Gavilar, Toh has a thick accent and speaks in a clipped, annoying way that sounds silly.
He has a keen interest in how the
Alethi
deal with
highstorms
, specifically with regard to just how the Alethi army does so when in the middle of a seige.
He finds
Soulcasters
to be fascinating because his people in Rira and
Iri
do not have them. And, he is impressed that there are so many
Shardblades
and so much Shardplate - perhaps half the world's wealth of Blades and Plate - all contained in the
Vorin
kingdoms
. He states that the
Heralds
themselves must favor the Alethi people.
Dalinar thinks him to be a sycophant, to which
Ialai
responds that he's a man without a home.
She tells Dalinar that the man has no loyalty; that he's at the mercy of those who'll take him in. Further, that he has only one piece he can play to secure his future (i.e., Evi's Shardplate).
She also says that while Toh and Evi want someone who can protect them, someone their family will be too afraid to attack, they'll want more than just asylum. They'll want to be part of things. Inject their blood into the royal line.
No man would ever mistake Toh for a soldier.
Beliefs
Toh believes that Gavilar's campaign has been impressive, noting that he sees a glint of the great
Sunmaker
in him.

.

PAGE: Took
CATEGORIES: Characters
URL: https://stormlightarchive.fandom.com/wiki/Took
======================================================================

Took
Biographical information
Ethnicity
Bavlander
Nationality
Bavlander
Gender
Male
Status
Dead
Occupation
Slaver
Appears in
The Way of Kings
Took
was one of
Szeth
's masters. He liked to think of himself as a seasoned traveler, but really was a transient worker, moving from town to town. Displaying Szeth was one of the ways he made quick friends. His greatest tale was of the time he had seen the
Nightwatcher
herself and stolen a
sphere
that glowed black at night.
*
Unlike some of Szeth's other masters, Took was not afraid of Szeth. He showed him off in one instance by directing Szeth to stand up, jump up and down, pour customer
Ton
's beer on his own head at an inn in
Bavland
.
Further, when Ton gripes to Took to ask of Szeth something else, Took tells Szeth to cut his arm up.
After Szeth complies, Took then tells him to cut his own throat, to which Szeth responds that he is forbidden from taking his own life because he is
Truthless
.
Took describes Szeth as being like a slave, only better he's a
Shinovar
. He says that Szeth doesn't run or talk back and he doesn't have to pay him either. That he's like a
parshman
, but smarter and worth a right many
spheres
.
That said, was unlikely that Took would be remembered, unless someone had noted his terribly exaggerated stories.
Later, Took was killed by footpads with a knife in
his
neck.
Notes
*
That tale always discomforted Szeth, as it reminded him of the strange black
sphere
Gavilar
had given him. He'd hidden that carefully in
Jah Keved
. He didn't know what it was, but he didn't want to risk a master taking it from him.

was the citylord of

He owned neither
Shardplate
nor a
Shardblade
. His allies were Brightlord

, his liege lord, and Brightlord Amaram, his distant cousin.
Following the death of Brightlord
Wistiow
in
1166
, Roshone was appointed as Hearthstone's citylord
as a part of his exile conditions.
According to
Kaladin
, Hearthstone was cursed with his presence, a man whose greedy ways had ruined far more than one life.
Roshone wasn't important enough to have stewardship over any of the
highprince
's lands - a distinction Kaladin had never realized while he lived in Hearthstone. Those lands were watched over by much more important lighteyes; Roshone was only a first point of contact with surrounding villages.
Appearance
He was described as a middle-aged man with a short, grey-streaked beard, and "a little too flabby to be a true soldier".
He wore a lighteyes' coat that is several seasons out of fashion, a wooden foot on his right leg, and had lost weight. His skin drooped on his figure like melted wax, bunching up at his neck.
He had the same imperious bearing, the same angry expression. To Kaladin, his light yellow eyes seemed to blame everyone and everything in this insignificant town for his banishment.
Later, the former citylord's cheeks hung in deflated jowls, still reflecting the weight he'd lost following his son's death seven years prior. Roshone had been ordered to shave his beard, perhaps because he'd been fond of it, and he no longer wore his proud warrior's
takama
. That had been replaced by the kneepads and short trousers of a
crem
scraper.
Before Exile
Roshone was a petty man, yet had become a trusted friend and advisor to a young King
Elhokar
. Roshone owned a few silversmith shops in
Kholinar
and disliked having competition. Using his connections to the king, he was able to convince Elhokar to jail several of his competitors on trumped up charges. Two of these competitors, who were the
grandparents
of
Moash
, a
Bridge Four
soldier, died in prison because Elhokar simply forgot about them.
In discovering this treachery,
Dalinar
originally wanted Roshone stripped of his station and made a tenner, forced to live his life in squalor. However, fearing the political repercussions, he tried to diminish the chance of a scandal by having Roshone tried for corruption and exiled to Hearthstone, a location he assumed would keep Roshone from doing further harm to the kingdom.
Time in Hearthstone
From the very beginning, Roshone took exception to Hearthstone's surgeon,
Lirin
. Roshone blamed Lirin for Wistiow's death and, by extension, causing his own exile to Hearthstone.
He suspected Lirin had stolen a large number of
spheres
from Wistiow. However, unable to prove it, he used his position and influence to encourage the townspeople to stop their donations to Lirin's family, forcing the family into poverty.
Kaladin described Roshone's influence over the people as "a confusing mixture of adoration and fear". Lirin further described Roshone as "unappeasable" and "the type who keeps kicking" once a man is down.
Roshone even suggested that if Lirin tried to escape Hearthstone, he would contact the nearby citylords and tell them of the accused theft, a threat Lirin did not find frightening given his position as a surgeon.
[citation needed]
Roshone and his son
Rillir
went on a
whitespine
hunt, where they were both badly injured. Lirin operated on them both and was unable to save Rillir.
Roshone blamed Lirin for the loss of his son and heir and forced Lirin's youngest son,
Tien
, into military service in revenge, using his connections with his cousin, Amaram. Kaladin joined Amaram's army as well, thinking to protect his brother.
Sometime later, Roshone became betrothed to
Laral Wistiow
. Their joining revealed that Lirin may had actually stolen the spheres though the legality of the matter was unclear; he had a previous agreement with Wistiow that he did not trust Roshone to honor.
Post-Everstorm
Hesina
promised Kaladin that things had gotten better since he left. That Roshone wasn't as bad as he once was. She thought he felt guilty.
But when Roshone initially acknowledged Kaladin's presence, he addressed him smugly, then decided to have a closer look at his brands, reaching over and holding up the hair in front of Kaladin's forehead.
“
"Storms, boy. What did you do? Hit a lighteyes?"
"Yes,"
Kaladin said.
Then punched him.
...
Roshone dropped like a felled tree.
"That,"
Kaladin said,
"was for my friend Moash."
”
Roshone sputtered on the ground, his nose bleeding, then instructed his guards to strike Kaladin down ... which they didn't do.
Afterward, Roshone sat apart from the others present, periodically pushing both hands along his flushed cheeks toward the back of his head in an odd nervous gesture. His nose had stopped bleeding, though he'd have a bruise.
Much later, Roshone was killed by Moash who jerked a knife in a quick, efficient slice, opening Roshone's throat and spilling his lifeblood across the front of his ragged clothing. Roshone fell to the ground and faded away in a pool of his own blood.
